text: The surgeon uses the da Vinci robot system SI to perform a prostatectomy on a patient positioned in a modified lithotomy position with a 30-degree Trendelenburg and right tilt. The dissection begins at the inferior mesenteric artery near the aorta, proceeding from a medial to lateral approach. Bipolar and monopolar energy devices are utilized, and surgical clips are applied to divide the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon begins dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery near the aorta using a medial to lateral approach, employing bipolar and monopolar energy devices. Surgical clips are applied to divide the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon and splenic flexure, performing an incision of the Girota fascia with monopolar scissors. The colorepiploid dissection is completed, and the splenic flexure is fully mobilized. The total mesorectal excision begins from the posterior plane of the mesorectum, followed by dissection of the right and left lateral planes, and concludes with the anterior plane.

The surgeon mobilizes the entire left colon and splenic flexure, performing an incision of the Girota fascia with monopolar scissors. The colopiploid dissection is completed, and the splenic flexure is mobilized. Total mesorectal excision is conducted, starting from the posterior plane of the mesorectum, followed by the right and left lateral planes, and concluding with the anterior plane.

The rectum is divided using a laparoscopic endogea device, which is recharged twice to facilitate a mechanical coloanal anastomosis. Prior to this, the urology team performs a radical prostatectomy and a large iliac lymphadenectomy, beginning the dissection from the right iliac artery.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic endogea device to divide the rectum, recharging the device twice to achieve a mechanical coloanal anastomosis. Concurrently, the urology team performs a radical prostatectomy and a large iliac lymphadenectomy, beginning the dissection at the right iliac artery.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the bladder neck and membranous urethra using two running V-lock sutures following radical prostatectomy. A surgical specimen is extracted through a 6 cm Pfannenstiel incision. After dividing the descending colon and sigmoid, a purse-string suture is placed to position a 29 mm anvil for an end-to-end anastomosis. The procedure concludes with the placement of a semi-aspirative drain and a diverting ileostomy. The surgical specimen shows complete resection of adenocarcinomas without nodal involvement.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the bladder neck and the membranous urethra using two running sutures with a B-lock. The surgical specimen is extracted through a six-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision. Following the division between the descending colon and the sigmoid, a purse-string suture is placed to position a 29-millimeter anvil for an end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the procedure with an end-to-end stapled anastomosis, places a semi-aspirative drain, and creates a diverting ileostomy. The surgical specimen reveals a complete resection of both adenocarcinomas with no nodal involvement.
